summary: Nadia argues that new licenses are unlikely to solve open source sustainability problems, both for practical and philosophical reasons. She observes that even GPL, despite decades of use, is often quietly ignored by companies, and recent relicensing attempts have faced strong community backlash. More fundamentally, she believes the focus should shift from controlling consumption (who uses the code) to capturing value on the production side (maintainer attention and access). Most users don't impose costs on maintainers—only those who engage with production do. She advocates for exploring patronage, sponsorships, and membership models rather than trying to 'stuff jack back into the box.'
key_points: GPL's enforcement has been inconsistent—even Linux kernel developers prefer negotiation over legal action against violators
Recent relicensing attempts (React, MongoDB, Redis) faced strong community opposition and were sometimes defeated
Most users are 'free riders' who impose no actual costs on maintainers since they just download and never interact
Value capture should focus on production (maintainer attention, access) rather than consumption (licensing restrictions)
Legal solutions to open source sustainability are a 'coping mechanism' that distract from building new models for the internet age
money_quotes: In other words, copyleft code 'attaches' itself to your code like a virus, infecting it with the same license, and I mean that in the best way possible.
While GPL has been adopted by many projects, it's unclear whether their users have respected its terms, nor whether anyone really cares if they don't.
While everyone is focused on putting up tollbooths, opportunities to 'price' maintainer attention, and access to maintainers, remain undervalued.
Most users don't actually impose costs back to maintainers: they download the code and never interact with the project.
Information, once freely shared, is a Pandora's box that cannot be closed again.
Instead of trying to stuff jack back into the box, let code run wild.

It's tempting to turn to legal options to manage this stuff, but I tend to view it as a coping mechanism, distracting us from the uncomfortable realization - perhaps felt collectively today - that regulation can't really rein in the internet after all.
key_insight: Rather than trying to restrict code consumption through licenses, open source sustainability efforts should focus on capturing value from the smaller group of users who actually engage with production and impose costs on maintainers.
